entries.text: I'm sharing the honest, technical, and
sometimes frustrating truth about
renting in Korea. Hi, my name is Honey.
I moved to Korea just last month from
the San Francisco Bay Area after living
in the US for 12 years. In the past
months, I have been deep in the
apartment hunting process and it has
been very eye-opening. Renting in the
Bay Area was pretty straightforward.
You'd either rent from a managed
apartment complex or you would rent from
a private owner who owns a single-family
house or a townhouse. And while the
prices are pretty expensive, it seems
pretty straightforward in general. Here
in Korea, the system is completely
different. Honestly, I haven't seen a
lot of people talking about these real
challenges when it comes to apartment
hunting in Korea. It's not always
choosing between two beautiful
apartments. There's so many hidden
things that we need to watch out for,
especially as a foreigner in a new
country. If you're thinking about moving
to Korea and want the real unfiltered
truth about how everything works here,
this video is for you. I hope it helps
you know what to look for and how to
better protect yourself. Without further
ado, let's dive in.
First and foremost, let's talk about
money. In Korea, when it comes to
renting, you're basically choosing
between two systems, wolse and jeonse.
Wolse is the closer one most foreigners
are used to. You pay a smaller deposit
up front and then you pay monthly rent.
And the deposit is usually 10 to 20
times of your monthly rent. So, when
you're scrolling through apps like
Zigbang or Dabang, you see two numbers
at the same time. It normally says 3,180
and then 1,100.
That normally means 30 million won is
your deposit and 1.8 million is your
monthly rent, basically. One thing worth
knowing is that you can sometimes
negotiate with the owner and a common
trade-off is that once you increase your
deposit by 10 million won, they decrease
the monthly rent by 50,000 won. Don't
forget to check kwalli b too. This is
the management fee that is added on top
of your monthly rent. A lot of the times
the kwalli b doesn't include utilities
like water, gas, electricity, etc. And I
have even seen some places that are
pretty nice but they charge up to like
won as kwalli b. So this is something
that you really need to factor in into
your budget. When I first did math, I
was pretty shocked at how expensive this
can get really quickly. I remember
thinking to myself, wait, what do you
mean I have to put like $30,000 down
just to rent a decent apartment here.
This made it really clear how important
it is to know your budget up front. Both
how much you're comfortable putting down
as deposit and what is your realistic
upper limit for your rent.
That alone can eliminate a lot of the
options for you. Chunsei is the unique
system that only exists here in Korea.
So basically you pay a large lump sum
deposit to the house owner and normally
this will be like 70% to 80% of the
house value
and you pay no monthly rent. A lot of
people take out loans for the deposit
and so you're basically paying interest
to the bank while the landlord uses your
money to invest in other things. I
personally chose wolse for a few
different reasons. First, I can't get a
loan yet in Korea because I don't have a
stable job history here. And second,
wolse gives me more flexibility in
managing my own finances which means I
don't risk putting like all of my money
into the house market here. And third
and most importantly is that there are
so many chunsei sagi in the news which
means deposit fraud and you risk not
getting like all your money back.
It just happens from time to time. For
me wolse just feels so much safer. Then
you might think, okay, then I will just
do 월세 because I'm guaranteed to have my
money back." But that's not entirely
true either because there are also
fraud regarding like 월세 deposit. I'll
talk about what to look for and how to
better protect yourself in a later
sections. There's also a common thing
that most people don't tell you about up
front is that you can't really be
looking for 월세 many months ahead because
the listings are just not out yet.
Ideally, you should be start looking 2
months ahead, but normally within a
month or even less. I personally find
this rule very frustrating because if
you can't find a place that you like
within that short window, it could mean
that you have nowhere to stay for a
certain period of time. It also forces
you to rush your decision without enough
time to compare options or gather enough
data points. And since most leases are 1
to 2 years once you sign, I really want
to make sure that I'm not moving into a
place that I would absolutely end up
hating.
But within this system, you have to
really line up your timeline perfectly
and I have a feeling that this just
won't always work out for everybody.
If you do end up having a few weeks of
gaps between right now and your moving
date, I use this app called 33 m².
It basically works like a Korean version
of Airbnb,
but more designed for Korean local
people who are looking for a short-term
stay for whatever reason. So for this
app, you can only choose to stay for a
unit of weeks, like 1 week, 2 weeks, 3
week, but you can't do like 1 day or 2
day. Here, let's say I want to stay in
Gangnam. So this is one example of a
property of a officetel in Gangnam. And
if you scroll down, you say start lease
and let's just choose a random date. And
that's how much you'll be paying for a
whole week. Now, this is the officetel I
rented from this app for a couple of
weeks. It is It's a really good
location, close to everything, very
clean and minimal, and a lot cheaper
than Airbnb for sure.
And now I want to talk about mindset
while looking for a place to stay here
in Korea. What worked perfectly for
other people might not work for you at
all because we all have different needs.
Maybe you need to be close to your
school or work. Maybe you're really on a
tight budget. Or maybe you want to
experience that modern loft style
apartment and don't mind it being
compact and smaller in size. All of
those are completely valid needs and
that's why it makes our apartment
hunting journey looks entirely
different. For me, I have three big
non-negotiables and this made my search
so much harder than expected and I'll
explain why each one is so important to
me. First up is space. I needed bigger
space, ideally over 30 square meters.
Seoul is a huge city with massive
population, so there are a lot of
officetels and one-rooms designed for
students and office workers. If you're a
student looking for one-room, it might
not be that hard to find. But for me,
after 12 years of living in the US, this
is actually my first year after such a
long time to be actually living in Asia.
This year I'm expecting lots of family
and friends visits, so I really need a
space that feels like separable and
comfortable enough for at least two
people to stay at the same time. So, my
first non-negotiable was clear, more
than 33 square meters and have at least
two rooms. Second, this place has to
allow cats. This single requirement
eliminates about 99% of the housing on
the market. I find it really interesting
because before moving to Korea, I
thought Korea is really pet-friendly.
You see pet cafes everywhere and so many
people walking their dogs outside, but
when it comes to apartment hunting, it
is an entirely different story. Most of
the time it just says no pets allowed
straight from the listing. And for the
ones that don't have it, if you like
message the real estate agent, they will
say, "Okay, sorry, no pets allowed." And
mind you, this is even before they show
me the property. And the irony is that
the nicer and better the place looks
like on the listing, the more likely
that they're going to have a strict no
pet policy. On the other hand, some
agents would quietly tell me, "Policies
wise, it's not allowed, but if the
landlord doesn't find out, you'll
probably be fine." But here's the thing,
regardless of what the agent says, your
contract will still say no pets allowed.
So, if the landlord ever finds out, you
could face eviction, a huge cleaning
fee, or lose part of your deposit. And
you're technically in the wrong. So,
personally, I don't recommend renting a
place like this if it specifically says
no pets allowed on the contract. At one
point in my search, I thought I had
found my perfect apartment. It has two
separate rooms and was a loft style
apartment. I usually don't like loft
style apartments because you have to
sleep on the second floor and it can be
really uncomfortable. But for this one,
you're basically living on the first
floor and you can use second floor for
storage. And just imagine how fun it
would be for my cats to jump up and down
through the stairs. I basically
immediately fell in love and I was like,
"Okay, let's sign this one." So, as we
were like talking to the agent and she
was like calling to the house owner,
then we found out that the owner doesn't
actually want cats in his apartment. We
tried negotiating and convince the house
owner, but it didn't work and in the end
we had to let it go. So, if you're
considering bringing your pets to Korea,
I'm just letting you know this is what
the situation might look like. Third
non-negotiable is budget. I set a clear
budget for myself. No more than 2
million won per month with everything
included and no more than 15 million won
down as deposit. I could have looked at
better places that are over 2 million
won per month, but I chose not to
because I know myself too well. There
are always better apartments and I could
easily go over budget. Since I don't
have a job in Korea yet, I want to make
sure that I spend my savings in a
reasonable way. In addition to these
three big non-negotiables, I also have
some preferred conditions. I'll print it
out here for you, but it's basically
fully furnished, some natural lighting,
not a basement or first floor, and
walking distance to some convenient
subway lines. All in all, these are nice
to haves. I was ready to compromise on
any of them as long as my three big
non-negotiables were met. If you're
planning to rent in Korea, I highly
recommend sorting out your own
non-negotiables and nice-to-haves early
on because it makes your whole search so
much easier and avoid wasting time on
places that won't work for you. Okay, so
after tons of searching and apartment
touring, let's say you finally land on a
place that you want to sign for. Before
you put down any money, there are a few
things you should check before signing
the contract. First, always look at
deunggi budeungbon, the official
property registry abstract. This is
something that I don't see a lot of
people talking about, but it can be
extremely important for foreigners who
are new to the Korea renting system.
We've all heard about jeonse sagi, but
wolse sagi exists, too. Your deposit,
whether it is like 10 million won or 50
million won, isn't a small amount of
money to lose, and some scams especially
target this type of money. I learned
this from the real estate agent and my
Korean family members. If the registry
shows that the property is under
shintak, you need to be extra careful.
What this means is that the apartment
doesn't belong to an individual or owner
in the usual way. It's owned or managed
by a trust company. They might control
multiple units or even the whole
building. In these cases, you absolutely
must get the official consent from the
trust company. Without proper consent
and documentation, your rights as a
tenant can be weak if problems arise.
Another reason to check the registry is
to see how much debt the owner has on
the property. For example, I saw one
place that looked really nice. The
property was around 200 million won, but
owner had over 800 million won in debt.
This is a classic gangtong situation.
Gangtong literally means empty tin can.
In real estate, it describes a property
where the total debt is higher than the
actual market value of the house. So,
there's basically nothing left if things
go south. This is a major red flag. If
the owner faces financial trouble, goes
bankrupt, or the property gets
auctioned, there may not be enough money
to return your deposit after paying the
bank and other creditors. So, you could
lose part of it or all of it, even worse
worse it.
So, always compare the total debt to the
real value of the house. If the debt is
much higher than the property worth,
it's best to walk away.
And when you're unsure, AI is your best
friend.
In addition to deunggi budeungbon, you
also need to make sure your jeonip singo
and hwakjeongilja are properly in place.
As I'm editing this video, I realized
that I've said a lot of negative things
about renting in Korea. I'm not saying
all renting here in Korea is a scam, but
this type of information just aren't
talked about enough, especially for
foreigners who just moved to Korea. And
we can only better protect ourselves if
we have done the due diligence and stuff
up front.
You can always ask help from the real
estate agents, but again, please please
do your own research.
It took me a whole month, but I finally
find my home for the next 2 years. If
you have any questions regarding renting
in Korea, leave me a comment below and
I'll try my best to answer. In the next
episode, I will try to take you through
moving day. Thank you for watching and
see you in the next one. Bye.
>> Mhm.
